title: Is There a Way to Break the Deadlock in Japan-China Relations?
gist: Japan-China relations have deteriorated since PM Takaichi Sanae's November 2025 remarks, and the author argues the deterioration may become a durable 'new normal' that Japan must manage rather than reverse, with no real thaw likely before China's 2027 Party Congress personnel changes. (Text is a truncated teaser ending in '[...]'.)

insights.claim: The author argues that, given the structure of China's foreign policy and US-China and Japan-China dynamics, the current deterioration in Japan-China relations could become a durable 'new normal' rather than a temporary downturn.
insights.speaker: KAWASHIMA Shin
insights.so_what: A structurally frozen Japan-China relationship raises the baseline for trade, supply-chain, and regional-security friction in Northeast Asia.
insights.quote: there is a real possibility that the current deterioration will become the new normal

insights.claim: No dramatic change in Japan-China relations is likely until after personnel changes are finalized at the Chinese Communist Party's National Congress expected in fall 2027.
insights.speaker: KAWASHIMA Shin
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-year horizon before any meaningful thaw — useful for timing expectations on bilateral risk.
insights.quote: With personnel changes expected at the National Congress of the Communist Party of China in the fall of 2027, Japan-China relations are not likely to change dramatically

insights.claim: A Takaichi-Xi bilateral meeting at the November 2026 APEC summit is very unlikely even though both leaders are expected to attend.
insights.speaker: KAWASHIMA Shin
insights.so_what: Absence of a leader-level meeting at APEC would confirm the freeze persists into late 2026 — a marker to watch.
insights.quote: the likelihood of an actual bilateral meeting is very low
insights.locator: 'The APEC summit is scheduled for November 2026'
